title: What is JSON
instruction: JSON is the universal language of web APIs. `json.Marshal` turns a Go struct into JSON bytes. Run this and see a struct become JSON.
successMessage: A Go struct became JSON. Notice the keys match the field names exactly. We'll fix that with struct tags next.

title: Struct tags — control the JSON keys
instruction: API consumers expect lowercase JSON keys. Add `json:"..."` tags to all three fields of `Book` so the output uses lowercase keys.
hint: Title string `json:"title"`  Author string `json:"author"`  Pages int `json:"pages"`

codeChecks.message: Add json:"title" tag to the Title field.
Add json:"author" tag to the Author field.
Add json:"isbn" tag to the ISBN field.
Replace the TODO comments with your struct tags.

successMessage: Lowercase keys — exactly what a REST API expects. Struct tags are how Go bridges the gap between Go naming and JSON naming conventions.

title: omitempty — skip empty fields
instruction: `omitempty` tells the JSON encoder to skip a field if it is its zero value. Add `omitempty` to the `Description` and `Edition` tags and see what each book produces.
expectedOutput: {"title":"Clean Code","description":"A handbook of agile software craftsmanship","edition":1}

codeChecks.message: Add omitempty to the Description tag: json:"description,omitempty"
Add omitempty to the Edition tag: json:"edition,omitempty"
Replace the TODO comments with the updated tags.

successMessage: 1984 has no description or edition in the output — omitempty dropped them. Clean catalog responses with no unnecessary nulls.

title: Unmarshal — JSON to struct
instruction: `json.Unmarshal` parses JSON bytes and fills a Go struct. Unmarshal the second JSON string into a `Book` and print its title, author, and pages.
hint: var b Book  err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(jsonStr2), &b)  fmt.Println(b.Title, b.Author, b.Pages)

codeChecks.message: Unmarshal jsonStr2 into a Book struct.
Replace the TODO comment with your unmarshal code.

successMessage: JSON string → Go struct. The tags map "title" → Title, "author" → Author automatically. This is how every Go HTTP handler reads request bodies.

title: Nested structs — author details
instruction: Nested Go structs become nested JSON objects automatically. Add an `AuthorInfo` struct with `FirstName`, `LastName`, and `BirthYear` fields (all tagged). Nest it inside `Book`.
hint: type AuthorInfo struct { FirstName string `json:"first_name"`; LastName string `json:"last_name"`; BirthYear int `json:"birth_year"` }  Book has Author AuthorInfo `json:"author"`

codeChecks.message: Define an AuthorInfo struct.
Add FirstName field to AuthorInfo.
Add LastName field to AuthorInfo.
Add BirthYear field to AuthorInfo.
Replace the TODO comments with your code.

successMessage: Nested struct → nested JSON object. The author info is cleanly grouped under its own key.

title: Maps — dynamic catalog metadata
instruction: `map[string]T` becomes a JSON object with dynamic keys. Add an entry `"language": "en"` to the metadata map and see it appear in the JSON.

codeChecks.message: Add metadata["language"] = "en" before marshaling.
Replace the TODO comment with your code.

successMessage: Maps become JSON objects with their keys as field names. Perfect for dynamic data where you cannot define a struct upfront.

title: MarshalIndent — pretty print library inventory
instruction: `json.MarshalIndent` produces human-readable JSON with line breaks and indentation. Switch from `json.Marshal` to `json.MarshalIndent` with two-space indentation.

codeChecks.message: Use json.MarshalIndent(lib, "", "  ") for pretty printing.
Replace the TODO comment with your MarshalIndent call.

successMessage: Readable, indented library inventory. Use this for log files, config files, and debugging.

title: Build a library catalog search response
instruction: Build a reusable catalog response wrapper: define a `CatalogResponse` struct with `Status`, `Query`, `Count`, and `Books` fields. Write `found()` and `notFound()` functions. Use `MarshalIndent` for pretty output.
hint: func found(query string, books interface{}) string { r := CatalogResponse{Status: "ok", Query: query, Count: ...}; b, _ := json.MarshalIndent(r, "", "  "); return string(b) }

codeChecks.message: Define a CatalogResponse struct.
Add json:"status" tag to the Status field.
Add json:"count" tag to the Count field.
Use omitempty on the Books field.
Define a found() function.
Define a notFound() function.
Use json.MarshalIndent for pretty-printed output.
Set status to "ok" in the found function.
Set status to "not_found" in the notFound function.

successMessage: A reusable library catalog search response wrapper. You just built the foundation of a library API.
